data.title: BNY Mellon Global Fixed Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
It seems likely that the actions of the US administration will be a guiding impulse on growth and inflation. As US import tariffs take hold, economic theory suggests they are likely to create higher US inflation and dampen growth outside the US.

Market Review

The first quarter of 2025 was centered around the effects and intentions of the new US administration, as Donald Trump resumed the presidency, with announcements having the effect of introducing material economic and market uncertainty. The on/off imposition of import tariffs on US trade partners became a focus for many market participants as they attempted to identify the way ahead for global economies, central bank actions and markets generally. The ongoing US policy pivots have continued to heavily influence policy pronouncements in many other countries, not least in Europe, where greater government commitments to spending on defense have been pushed up within domestic agendas. The US Federal Reserve was alone among the major central banks in not changing interest rates, with the European Central Bank, the Banks of England and Canada, the Swiss Central Bank, and the Reserve Bank of Australia all cutting rates, while the Bank of Japan raised rates during the quarter.

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended March 31, 2025, the Fund’s Class I shares returned 1.24%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund’s unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg Global Aggregate USD Hedged Index, returned - 1.17% for the same period.

Market Review (continued)

Government bond markets were mixed across the first quarter. News, events and reactions were largely centered on the announcements and policies of the incoming US presidential administration. The threat of tariffs led to concerns that the US might be more exposed to recession, pushing 10-year US Treasury yields lower by 36 basis points (bp). In Germany, plans to expand government spending on infrastructure and defense through extra borrowing helped drive 10-year yields up by 37bp. Meanwhile, 10-year UK gilt yields rose by 11bp as concerns remained that inflation may not yet be beaten. In Japan, expectations that the Bank of Japan will continue to raise interest rates while inflation continued to rise sent 10-year government bond yields up 39bp, briefly moving above 1.5% for the first time since the global financial crisis late in the quarter. Yield levels in the emerging markets declined slightly, with the JP Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index yield falling 9bp to 6.30%.

With the exception of euro investment grade corporates, credit markets were generally weaker than government markets in the quarter, as spread levels typically widened. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) over governments for the Bloomberg US Corporate Index ended the quarter 14bp wider at 94bp, with the related Bloomberg US Credit Index 12bp wider. By contrast, the spread on the Bloomberg Euro Corporate Index tightened by 4bp, but that should be viewed in combination with the increase in German government yields during the period. The Bloomberg Sterling Agg Corporate Index was 15bp wider. In high-yield markets, the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Index spread was 60bp wider, reflecting the deterioration in risk markets over the quarter. Meanwhile, the Bloomberg Pan-European High Yield Index spread widened by 25bp.

Performance Review

The Fund outperformed the benchmark in the period. Active rates positioning outperformed on the quarter, driven by an underweight in 10-year Japanese government bonds and an overweight to 10-year US Treasuries. Curve positioning provided outperformance driven by steepening in the US. Cross-market trades underperformed on the quarter, with an underweight Canada versus the US, an overweight New Zealand versus the US, and an underweight Sweden versus the euro were the primary performance headwinds. Inflation positioning provided material outperformance driven by falling UK inflation expectations. This was partially countered by underperformance associated with relative value positioning between US and euro break evens.

Credit faced headwinds this quarter as a small overweight to credit beta drove slight underperformance. Mortgage-backed securities (MBS) also saw marginal underperformance during the quarter, countered by security selection, where outperformance was driven by an overweight to ROMANI and European banks. Finally, foreign currency positioning slightly underperformed driven by an underweight Swedish krona IRS and euro. The performance impact of these positions was partially countered by overweights to the US dollar and yen.

There were several changes to the active risk profile of the strategy during the quarter. The strategy increased topline duration by +1 years to +.5 years. The strategy net bought duration on the long end of the curve in Japan, 10yr Canada and 5yr Swedish krona IRS. These buys were against sales of euro, Australian dollar, and Chinese yuan denominated duration. In curve positioning the strategy reduced the steepener in US dollars. In credit beta, the strategy added 0.375 units of risk in the quarter, primarily in euro-denominated investment-grade credit. The strategy sold cash investment exposure and moved overweight synthetic risk. Finally, from an asset allocation standpoint, changes during the quarter were to increase euro Investment Grade Risk versus US dollar. In foreign exchange, the strategy bought Swiss franc, euro, Australian dollar, Canadian dollar, and New Zealand dollar while selling US dollar, Swedish krona, Norwegian krona, Chinese yuan, and yen.

Market Outlook

It seems likely that the actions of the US administration will be a guiding impulse on growth and inflation. As US import tariffs take hold, economic theory suggests they are likely to create higher US inflation and dampen growth outside the US. Retaliatory tariffs elsewhere could put upward pressure on inflation in the other countries and squeeze US exports. Overall effects may be difficult to quantify, and the timeframes for those effects will be equally challenging to predict. Higher inflation generally may make monetary policymakers reticent to ease interest rates further, which could have an additional stifling effect on activity. Additionally, geopolitical events and uncertainty remain key concerns for many investors, with the conflict in Ukraine still showing no definitive signs of ending.

While the imposition of import tariffs could see some switch in demand to domestically produced goods or relocating of production into the US, any reciprocal tariffs could have detrimental effects on US exports. Inflation is expected to remain around current levels and struggle to reach the target level, remaining at or just above 2.5%, while tariffs will not help the slow descent of inflation towards the 2% target level. We believe growth will be around 2.0% this year, decelerating slightly to around 1.7% in 2026. The Federal Reserve is likely to ease policy only slowly, with two rate cuts of 25bp in 2025 and two more in 2026, in our view. Policymakers could halt easing completely if inflation rises, even with a weaker economy, until they believe price pressures are back under control. Despite the risks of higher inflation, we believe the prospect of softer growth will see 10-year US Treasury yields fall back in the next year to around 3.90%.

Prospects for growth in the eurozone continue to be soft given the impacts of US tariffs, although the anticipated fiscal expansion in Germany and potentially elsewhere could help offset that drag. The eurozone is well ahead of other regions on the path toward sustainably achieving target inflation, and the ECB is likely to continue its easing policy. We now expect rates to be brought down to 2% by the end of 2025, remaining there for some time thereafter. Given the recently approved changes permitting Germany to expand its borrowing on both infrastructure and defense spending we have raised our expectations for German government bond yields. In 12 months, we now see 10-year German yields at 2.55% and longer-dated yields closer to 3%.

In the emerging markets, China’s domestic demand remains soft due to the ongoing drag from the property sector and weak household and business confidence. We do, however, expect improvements in 2025. The external sector will likely move from a tailwind to a headwind as US tariffs dampen exports. China's 5% growth target appears unlikely to be achieved, and downsides remain. The picture across other emerging markets is mixed, with individual nations facing differences in the levels and extent of US tariffs. As a key provider to the US, Mexico may be one of the harder-hit countries, affecting its growth prospects more than others. The path of inflation is otherwise still generally downward, which, in the absence of pressure on currencies, could provide individual central banks with the capacity to continue easing policy or, as in the case of Brazil, to begin reversing recent rate increases.

Risks

Bonds are subject to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries. High yield bonds involve increased credit and liquidity risk than higher-rated bonds and are considered speculative in terms of the issuer's ability to pay interest and repay principal on a timely basis. The use of derivatives involves risks different from, or possibly greater than, the risks associated with investing directly in the underlying assets. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid, and difficult to value and there is the risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the portfolio will not correlate with the underlying instruments or the portfolio's other investments.

The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index (Unhedged) is a flagship measure of global investment grade debt from twenty-four local currency markets. This multi-currency benchmark includes treasury, government-related, corporate and securitized fixed-rate bonds from both developed and emerging markets issuers. J.P. Morgan Market Bond indices are a family of benchmarks designed to track the performance of various bond markets, including those in the US, emerging markets, and global markets. These indices cover a range of asset classes and currencies, providing investors with a way to track and compare the performance of different bond sectors. The Bloomberg US Corporate Index is a benchmark for the investment-grade, fixed-rate, taxable US corporate bond market. It includes USD-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility, and financial issuers. The Bloomberg Euro Corporate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that tracks the performance of the investment-grade, euro-denominated, fixed-rate corporate bond market. It includes securities from industrial, utility, and financial issuers publicly issued in Eurobond and eurozone domestic markets. The Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index measures the USD-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. The Bloomberg Sterling Aggregate Corporate Bond Index is a fixed-rate, investment-grade Sterling-denominated corporate bond index that tracks the performance of the fixed-rate, investment-grade Sterling-denominated corporate bond market. It's part of a broader family of fixed income indices offered by Bloomberg. The index includes bonds from industrial, utility, and financial issuers. The Bloomberg Pan-European High Yield Index tracks the performance of fixed-rate, high-yield corporate bonds issued in Europe, denominated in EUR, GBP, CHF, DKK, NOK, and SEK. It specifically focuses on non-investment grade debt, excluding emerging market debt and certain other debt types. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

Inflation is the rate of increase in the cost of living. Inflation is usually quoted as an annual percentage, comparing the average price this month with the same month a year earlier. Relative Value, which is a measure of a stock's long-term price appreciation potential compared to a risk-free investment like AAA corporate bonds. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) is the measurement of the spread of a fixed-income security rate and the risk-free rate of return, which is then adjusted to take into account an embedded option. A basis point (bp) is a unit of measure used to indicate percentage changes in financial instruments. Basis points are typically expressed with the abbreviations "bp," "bps," or "bips." One basis point is equal to 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%. In decimal form, one basis point appears as 0.0001 (0.01/100). ROMANI refers to Return on Market Value of Equity (ROME). ROME is a metric used to assess a company's ability to generate profits relative to its market value. It's a useful tool for value investors, helping them identify potentially undervalued stocks.

data.title: SOBO: A Stable Oil Pipeline Dividend Play
data.description:
Summary

South Bow Corporation (NYSE:SOBO) is a pure-play crude oil pipeline company that owns the Keystone Pipeline System that carries ~620Mbbl/d. The company has a strong moat and a secured dividend. It also has cash flow optionality and trading at fair value to its peers. I recommend a BUY due to its secured dividend yield and strong business moat despite having a concentration risk.

Business Model

Essentially, SOBO owns pipelines that transport crude oil from Canada to big refineries in the US Midwest and Gulf Coast. Their keystone pipeline is the ~4,900km crude oil pipeline that runs from Alberta to Illinois, Oklahoma and Texas.

The contract is arranged under a "Take-or-Pay" long-term agreement. This means that even if there is no crude oil being transported across the pipelines, SOBO would still receive the flat fee based upon the contract agreement. This means the volatility of oil price does not impact the company's revenue much. It serves mainly as a transportation piece of the supply chain. You can think of it as like a toll-road business model, where customers pay to access the infrastructure. As its cost are pretty much fixed, this makes SOBO's revenue resilient and has predictable margins.

SOBO also has a smaller marketing division that profits from the price arbitrage between Alberta where oil is traded at a discount and sold to the US Gulf where prices are higher. The reason for the discount is when supply production exceeds capacity, forcing Canadian oil producers to pull their prices down by selling locally. Moreover, the oil produced there is heavy and sour which is rich in sulfur and more expensive to refine than the light sweet crude alternative.

This means that the barrier to entry is very high, as it is very difficult to create another pipeline that spreads across from Canada to the US. There is only another pipeline, Enbridge's Mainline system, that transports heavy crude into the US from Canada. Thus, being in a duopoly environment, the possibility of price competition is so low and the incentive to do so is small as only these two players could move heavy Canadian crude to the US Gulf.

Investment Thesis

By investing SOBO, you are effectively owning a decent and covered dividend yield of 8%. The dividend is covered by ~1.5x free cash flow. Moreover, it has a strong moat that is hard to replicate and would take billions of dollars to build a cross-border pipeline.

Moreover, the company, despite being part of the oil supply chain, is less impacted by the oil price volatility due to its "take-or-pay" contract.

Other than a modest growth project of building a 25km feeder pipeline of C$180m, the company has limited CAPEX needs and this frees up cash flow for the company to return cash to shareholders, which is what they are doing now.

Thus, the company could use this cash to either reduce its leverage, which would strengthen its balance sheet and lead to a higher re-rating, or they could do a share buyback if management deem it undervalued. I like the optionality and predictability business model, making it a wonderful business to own for the dividend as well.

Valuation

Doing a peer multiple valuation, SOBO trades around 10.5x forward EV/EBITDA and this is similar to its peer Pembina at 10.4x and slightly lower than Enbridge at 12.4x. Part of the discount is because of SOBO's single-asset risk, and its slightly higher leverage compared to Enbridge.

As the company only spun off recently, there could also be uncertainty where investors are still waiting on the sideline for the full-year results to be released before gaining conviction. Even if the company doesn't seem to be massively undervalued unlike my other articles, the company has many good qualities, such as a strong moat and an attractive duopoly landscape and a stable high dividend yield which would attract income investors.

Risk

Its most obvious risk is the concentration risk from its Keystone pipeline. As it is a not a diversified midstream operator, ~90% of its EBITDA is tied to one-system. This means that one outage could lead to a severe de-rating. A way to think about the risk is that SOBO's network operates as a single spine from Hardisty to Houston. They have no parallel crude-export pipelines in the system, and so any outage in any given part would pause the oil flow. But with that said, the fees they are collecting are stable and flat and are not dependent on volume flows. Management has also said that any cleanup or repair cost is largely covered by insurance. Thus, this would mitigate and offset the downside risk.

Moreover, pipeline capacity growth such as the Trans Mountain expansion could shift where producers transport their crude oil. However, SOBO's contracts are long-term and so their revenue is protected until early 2030s.

Conclusion

Even with its concentration risk, I am still comfortable owning the company because of its good qualities and strong moat. Although this investment idea's upside is also not as significant as my other more "contrarian" ideas, I feel this idea can also be a good idea for income investors who prefer a steady, secure dividend. Thus, I rate this as a Buy.

data.title: From Tokyo To Your Wallet: How Japan's Monetary Shift Is Shaping Your Financial Future
data.description:
Japanese monetary policy: then and now

Japan's recent economic history is something that is not covered in finance books, a sort of experiment that has lasted for decades. Negative interest rates, yield curve control, a debt-to-GDP ratio of over 200% and a currency that is constantly losing value. It seems like the perfect recipe for disaster, but this is Japan. Or rather, this was Japan until recently.

In March 2024, there was a historic change when the Bank of Japan raised short-term interest rates by a few basis points, just enough to bring them out of negative territory (where they had been since 2016). In addition, radical yield curve control for the 10-year Japanese government bond interest rate was abolished. Until then, the Bank of Japan had prevented the 10-year bond yield from rising above certain pre-set thresholds. How did it do this?

Simply put, given its unlimited economic power, it bought as many bonds as possible to bring the rate below the set threshold: anyone who went against the Bank of Japan would lose money. This is an extreme expansionary monetary policy, unique among developed economies, and was aimed at making it cheaper for Japanese people to borrow money.

The Bank of Japan has tried everything possible to achieve economic growth, something that has been lacking for too long. First because of the bursting of the bubble in the 1990s and then with the great financial crisis.

Japan's real GDP has grown too little compared to its 2008 peak, and deflation has been a constant drag on its economy. The Bank of Japan's target is 2% inflation, but in over two decades it has rarely achieved this. There have been more years of deflation, the worst enemy of economic growth. After all, why buy something today if it will cost less tomorrow? This vicious circle leads to postponed consumption and consequently slows down the economy.

These problems, combined with a clear demographic decline and the highest proportion of elderly people in the world (28% of the population is over 65), have led the country into a decades-long period of stagnation. This is why Japan, despite decades of ultra-expansionary monetary policy, has never experienced particularly negative effects on inflation. Debt has increased, but the purchasing power of the Japanese people has remained stable.

Think about if another economy had done something like this; inflation would probably have risen well above double digits. Its demographic and economic dynamics make Japan unique, but what happened in March 2024 may have marked a turning point.

The possible consequences of Japan's new monetary policy

Japan with negative interest rates and endless purchases of 10-year bonds no longer exists, as inflation is kicking in for the first time in decades.

2022 was generally a year of high inflation almost everywhere in the world, including Japan when compared to previous years. But why is Japan's inflation rate still well above the 2% target?

The major central banks at that time raised interest rates much more than the Bank of Japan did, and this typically has a significant impact on the currency market.

The currencies of the countries from which Japan imports the most have appreciated against the yen, making it more expensive for the country to import. At its peak, this process saw the yen depreciate by as much as 30-40% in just a few years, which is unusual given that the yen was historically considered a safe-haven asset. Imports of energy and especially food (rice above all) are having the greatest impact on the inflation rate.

Higher-than-target inflation is leading the Bank of Japan to adopt a restrictive monetary policy after a long time, and the limits of the past that set a ceiling on bond yields no longer exist.

Today, the bond market no longer has any limits to respect, but at the same time it shows a degree of uncertainty that was not present a few years ago. Without the Bank of Japan buying debt regardless, we do not know how far Japanese rates will go.

We are mindful that such price rises are having a negative impact on people's livelihood and consumption. If our forecast materialises, we will continue to raise our policy rate. But there is extremely high uncertainty over the outlook for each country's trade policy and its fallout. As such, we will determine without pre-conception whether the economy and prices move in line with our forecast.

Central bank's deputy governor Shinichi Uchida

It seems that the intention is to raise them further, and the surge in 40-year bond yields in recent weeks is quite impressive. The increase in Japanese interest rates, this time not followed by other central banks, is causing the yen to appreciate, thereby normalizing import costs.

But why is this relevant to the finances of a US citizen? The reason lies in the influence that Japanese rates have on US rates.

The world had become accustomed to Japan's negative interest rates and exploited this situation to its advantage through carry trade. In practice, this consists of borrowing money at low-interest rates in a given currency, converting it, and investing in more profitable assets denominated in the converted currency.

Carry trade, borrowing yen and investing in US bonds, created spreads of over 5% and was not the only source of profit. A continuously depreciating yen also allowed investors to earn money when converting their proceeds. This was a very profitable financial transaction considering that it involved high leverage.

The latter is both the strength and weakness of the carry trade. As long as interest rates were low, and the yen was depreciating, the cycle was fueled, but when the Bank of Japan became hawkish (at the end of July 2024), investors partially lost interest in carry trade because it no longer generated the same risk/return profile.

On August 5, 2024, the market suffered a sharp collapse and the yen appreciated dramatically due to the forced closure of many leveraged transactions. That day can be compared to a kind of earthquake, a shock for the financial world, but it is not over yet because the tsunami may still be to come.

The fact that the yen is appreciating against the dollar and that its interest rates continue to rise makes carry trade, or more generally the purchase of Treasury Bonds, less attractive: Japanese investors are now more inclined to buy their own country's government bonds rather than US ones.

Japan is currently the foreign country with the largest holdings of US debt, accounting for approximately 3.13% of the total. If Japanese demand for US debt declines, T-Bond yields could face upward pressure. This is even more pronounced in the event of debt sales, an important lever that Japan can use to defend itself against tariffs.

Japan has long been the largest buyer of US debt, and the historic shift in its monetary policy could change the certainties of the past. In my view, the US can no longer rely on Japan to sell its debt, and the same is true for China, albeit for different reasons.

China has been reducing its exposure to US debt for years, but together with Japan it owns 5.30% of the total US debt. Not having these countries available to sell its debt means lower demand for US T-Bonds and consequently higher interest rates. Furthermore, beyond the purely numerical issue, when two of the world's leading economies stop buying your debt, it does not send a good signal to the market.

Other countries could compensate for weak demand from Japan and China, but take a look at this chart.

Countries such as the UK, Canada, the Cayman Islands, and Luxembourg have significantly increased their exposure to US debt, but these are mainly private investors driven to buy for purely speculative reasons: a private individual has no political interests and does not have large volumes at their disposal. How do I know this? Foreign investors held $9.04 trillion in March 2025, an increase of 11.61% over last year, but the amount held by Foreign Officials (central banks and foreign government institutions) rose by only 2.83%. This means that the entities that can provide stability to US debt are playing an increasingly marginal role. If private investors no longer find the risk/return ratio attractive, they sell US debt without too much trouble. This does not happen with a central bank because profit is not the only reason it bought it.

Furthermore, the increase in US debt holdings by foreign officials hides a pitfall. In fact, holdings of T-Bonds & Notes have decreased by 1.25%, while those of T-Bills have increased by 57.22%. This is a clear sign that central banks and foreign government institutions are losing confidence in US debt, and when they buy, they do so in the short term to minimize risk.

Conclusion

Japan has been one of the main buyers of US debt for years, and its current exposure is so large that it will probably remain the largest foreign holder of US debt for a long time to come. However, after decades of ultra-expansionary monetary policy, inflation has hit Japan too, forcing the Bank of Japan to rethink its past certainties.

With interest rates rising and the yen no longer depreciating as it did in the past, Japanese investors have more incentive than before to buy Japanese debt rather than US debt; carry trade is no longer as profitable as it once was and is now riskier.

Japan was one of the main buyers of US debt, but after the change in monetary policy, I expect it will no longer play a key role in this regard. Together with China, they hold more than 5% of total US debt, and this leverage could be used to negotiate against tariffs. If China and Japan stop buying US debt, it is not an encouraging sign for other countries.

US debt is increasingly passing into the hands of private investors, but they are too volatile. Focused solely on profit, they are not a stable source of debt financing unlike foreign central banks. To keep risk low, the latter have preferred to buy T-Bills rather than long-term government bonds over the past year.

All this, combined with a growing fiscal deficit and a foreign policy that is causing turmoil due to tariffs, is not helping the US's credibility. In fact, long-term bond yields are soaring and have exceeded 5% for 20-year bonds.

Trillions of dollars will need to be refinanced in the coming years, but to avoid the mistake of the previous administration, the average maturity of the debt must be extended by focusing on long maturities. However, interest rates are rising, and the US is at serious risk of paying billions and billions more in interest.

Returning to the title of the article, this situation also directly affects US citizens. If rates do not fall, they will pay more on their mortgages and credit card interest. Furthermore, if the government has to pay more interest, it will be forced to cut public spending (education, defense, and so on).

There is an urgent need to find buyers for the debt who can bring rates down, but as we have seen, Japan could pull out and China has already been doing so for several years. Who will buy US debt? That is the million-dollar question.

Finally, I would like to conclude this article with a rather interesting chart concerning the stock market; Japanese monetary policy does not only impact the bond market.

For years, there has been a fairly strong correlation between the S&P 500 and USD/JPY, particularly since 2024; in some cases, it is so strong that it exceeds 0.90x. In practice, when the yen appreciates against the dollar, the market falls. If the Bank of Japan continues to raise rates, I expect the yen to continue to appreciate against the dollar, and if the correlation holds, the S&P500 should see a drawdown. So, a stronger yen may not only deteriorate demand for US bonds but also the US stock market.

These are just assumptions, but I think it is interesting to note how interconnected the global economy is. An increase in interest rates in Japan can ultimately have consequences for the variable-rate mortgage or investments of a citizen of another country on the other side of the world. Globalization has brought everything together, and a Japan that no longer follows the dynamics of past decades is currently a wild card for the world economy.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Volatility's Back, And It's Wreaking Havoc On Metal Sourcing Strategies
data.description:
Original Post

By Stuart Burns

Speculators may love volatility, but metal buyers hate it. As for Trump Tariffs 2025, the jury is still out. The truth is that a bout of unexpected turbulence like we've seen over the last couple of months can knock even the best-laid strategies sideways. The reasons are clear: markets nosedived in March and hit bottom in early April as fears over tariffs and a broader hit to global trade shook confidence.

Much of this played out in real time, cargoes were diverted, and consumers scrambled for alternative sources of supply. But when the threat of tariffs began to fade, prices surged. Copper jumped from the mid-$8,000s to $9,500 on Wednesday, a 17% increase. After aluminum briefly plunged below $2,300 from over $2700, it rebounded to the mid-$2,450s. All base metals followed a similar pattern, with the wild gyrations leaving many buyers shell-shocked and unsure of what's coming next.

Markets, Metals and Trump Tariffs 2025

In the next few weeks, progress in tariff discussions and looming deadlines from the Trump administration to secure concessions will drive momentum. Many analysts remain pessimistic about demand prospects, expecting prices to dip in the second half of the year.

For example, JPMorgan projects that weak demand will pull prices down once the current furor fades, forecasting nickel at $14,000 and zinc at $2,500, a level even the April sell-off didn't hit. The bank also sees a surplus in both metals this year. Even copper, which boasts much tighter fundamentals, could ease in the second half. Finally, Reuters predicts that aluminum may retrace to April levels (around $2,325), especially given soft auto demand.

Pessimism vs. Reality

Analysts base much of this pessimism on expectations of a slowing global economy and disrupted trade flows triggered by tariffs meant to reset the U.S.'s trade terms with the world. However, both global growth and trade flows have shown surprising resilience. The chaos of the past two months will undoubtedly force consumers who rely too heavily on China to rethink their strategies, but we don't expect global growth to grind to a halt.

Inflation remains manageable, energy costs continue to moderate, aside from a few ideologically skewed markets like the UK, and both corporate balance sheets and bank lending remain strong. As for metals, demand still holds firm, even if it lags in some sectors like autos. As negotiations ease tensions and stock markets rebound, wallets will likely open, boosting demand later this year.

Fear drives pessimism. Right now, both are steering the markets. But that sets a lower bar for speculator expectations. Even a modest boost in sentiment could pull investors back in, and with them, rising prices. Ultimately, don't bet on "lower for longer."

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Dips
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 1.8 percentage points to 37.7%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the first time in 16 weeks and is above 30% for only the seventh time this year. Bullish sentiment was last higher on January 30, 2025 (41.0%).

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 5.9 percentage points to 25.6%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 44th time in 46 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 7.7 percentage points to 36.7%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 25th time in 27 weeks. Bearish sentiment was last lower on January 30, 2025 (34.0%).

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 9.5 percentage points to 1.0%. The bull-bear spread is above its historical average of 6.5% for the first time in 16 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they would describe the updated earnings guidance given by companies during the first-quarter 2025 earnings season.

Here is how they responded:

  • Better than I expected: 28.2%.
  • Approximately what I expected: 41.7%.
  • Worse than I expected: 11.5%.
  • Not sure/no opinion: 18.3%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 37.7%, up 1.8 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 25.6%, up 5.9 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 36.7%, down 7.7 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.
